one_line_takeaway: By injecting chat-template delimiter tokens into a single message, an attacker can forge an entire fake conversation history, and combining that with a log-likelihood-motivated 'answer-deferral via word substitution' trick (DIA-II) yields state-of-the-art black-box jailbreaks (~0.89 on Llama-3.1-8B, ~0.82 on GPT-4o) that also evade 5 defenses.
key_finding: DIA achieves state-of-the-art black-box ASRs, especially on recent, well-aligned models where prior attacks collapse. On AdvBench (LlamaGuard-2, single query) DIA-I reaches 0.906 on Gemma-2-9B while DIA-II reaches 0.654 on Llama-3.1-8B and 0.735 on Llama-3.1-70B and 0.528 on GPT-4o; by contrast DRA drops to 0.002 on Llama-3.1-8B and 0.000 on GPT-4o. Multi-query, after 10 iterations DIA attains 0.89 ASR on Llama-3.1-8B and 0.82 on GPT-4o (AdvBench), while DRA stays near zero on those models. DIA-I and DIA-II are complementary (DIA-I best on the Gemma-2 family; DIA-II best elsewhere, particularly on Llama-3.1 where prefilling appears patched). The paper's novel log-likelihood observation (deferred harmful answers score higher, Fig 4) underpins DIA-II. TIA reaches ~0.9 template-identification accuracy with NTmax=5 (about 10 queries). Against 5 defenses on Gemma-2-9B, DIA-I averages 0.93 DPR and DIA-II 0.82, both beating all baselines; the perplexity filter is entirely ineffective (DPR 1.000 for all methods) because black-box jailbreak prompts are highly readable. A counterintuitive secondary finding: for most families larger models are MORE vulnerable (ASR rises with size), attributed to the usefulness-vs-safety trade-off.
core_contribution: The paper introduces DIA (Dialogue Injection Attack), a black-box LLM jailbreak paradigm that manipulates the conversation HISTORY rather than a single prompt. Its central technical move is 'dialogue injection': because a chat frontend wraps user input in a chat template (role prefixes/suffixes such as Llama-3's <|start_header_id|>...<|eot_id|>), an attacker who knows the template can embed those delimiter tokens inside a single WebUI message to forge arbitrary prior turns, including fake assistant responses and a replaced system prompt, that the inference engine treats as genuine history (analogous to SQL injection). On top of this the authors build (1) DIA-I, a black-box adaptation of the gray-box prefilling attack that injects an affirmative response beginning plus a 'continue' command, using an Affirmative Beginning Generation Module (ABGM) that purges a malicious prompt into a benign one via a keyword->alternative mapping, answers it, then maps the answer's first sentence back to harmful terms so no aligned LLM ever produces harmful content; (2) DIA-II, built on the paper's own novel observation that a DEFERRED harmful response has higher log-likelihood than an immediate one, which forces the victim to first perform a word-substitution task (via a Similar Demonstration Generation Module, SDGM) so it emits benign text before answering, simultaneously deferring the answer and disguising the malicious prompt so the input contains no harmful words. Supporting contributions are a Template Inference Attack (TIA) that identifies which open-source chat template a victim uses by combining two candidate templates into a probe + 'repeat' command (relaxing the template-knowledge assumption), and a prompt-rewrite algorithm (addReason/reorder/simplify with a >=50% keyword-retention constraint) that boosts multi-query attacks.
threat_model: Strict black-box adversary with NO access to victim architecture, parameters, or training data. The attacker interacts only via (1) a chat API, or (2) a WebUI. In the WebUI case the attacker is assumed to know the victim's chat template; this assumption is relaxed by the template inference attack when the model uses a publicly available (open-source) template. Attacker goal follows the standard jailbreak objective: make the victim not merely fail to refuse but actually produce a reasonable, harmful answer to a malicious prompt. The attack exploits that (a) chat templates concatenate manipulable history into the model input, (b) shallow safety alignment only constrains the first few output tokens, and (c) alignment training data places malicious content in user (not assistant) turns.
limitations: 1) The core dialogue-injection vector requires the victim to accept and faithfully re-parse chat-template delimiter tokens supplied inside user text; models/frontends that sanitize or escape special role tokens would blunt it (the WebUI case also needs template knowledge, only relaxed for open-source templates via TIA). 2) DIA-I is a refinement of the known prefilling attack and is already weakened on Llama-3.1-8B (single-query 0.000 on AdvBench Guard-2), i.e., partly patched; the more robust contribution is DIA-II. 3) The defense-penetration evaluation is run on a SINGLE model (Gemma-2-9B) and one dataset (AdvBench), limiting generality of the DPR claims; the authors also explicitly exclude post-hoc detectors (LlamaGuard, ShieldGemma) citing streaming-SLO constraints, so DIA is not shown to bypass output-side guard models. 4) Success is measured only by LlamaGuard-2/3 classifiers, which have known miscalibration; no human validation of harmfulness/usefulness is reported. 5) The attack relies on long-context WebUIs accepting injected multi-turn text. 6) It is an offensive tool; the paper offers observations but no concrete defense.

datasets_used: AdvBench (520 harmful instructions with affirmative beginnings; mean 12.8 Llama-3 tokens)
HEx-PHI (330 harmful instructions; 30 examples x 11 prohibited categories; mean 31.5 Llama-3 tokens)
MaliciousInstruct (100 malicious instructions phrased as questions; mean 12.7 Llama-2 tokens)
NoRobots (10,000 entries; used only for the chat-template-transfer feasibility experiment)
quantitative_results: Single-query ASR on AdvBench (LlamaGuard-2): DIA-I = 0.906 on Gemma-2-9B (best), 0.877 on Gemma-2-27B, 0.571 on Llama-3-8B; DIA-II = 0.654 on Llama-3.1-8B, 0.735 on Llama-3.1-70B, 0.528 on GPT-4o, 0.467 on Llama-2-7B
Single-query ASR on AdvBench (LlamaGuard-3): DIA-II = 0.800 on Llama-3.1-8B, 0.885 on Llama-3.1-70B, 0.739 on GPT-4o; DIA-I = 0.983 on Gemma-2-9B
Multi-query: after 10 iterations DIA reaches ~0.89 ASR on Llama-3.1-8B and ~0.82 on GPT-4o (AdvBench); DIA-I after 10 iters >0.4 on Llama-3.1-8B and >0.5 on GPT-4o-mini (Guard-2)
Baseline collapse on hardened models: DRA single-query AdvBench = 0.002 on Llama-3.1-8B and 0.000 on GPT-4o (Guard-2); DeepInception/DRA ASR degraded 67%/99% on Llama-3.1-8B vs Llama-2-7B

Defense Pass Rate on AdvBench/Gemma-2-9B: DIA-I average 0.93 (OpenAI 1.000, Perplexity 1.000, Defensive System Prompt 1.000, Defensive Prompt Patch 0.985, Bergeron 0.651); DIA-II average 0.82 (Bergeron 0.690, best among methods)
Template Inference Attack accuracy reaches ~0.9 or higher at max-try NTmax=5 (i.e., ~10 WebUI queries), tested on Llama-3-8B, Gemma-2-9B, Qwen-2-7B, 200 inferences each
Template-transfer degradation: fine-tuning Gemma-2-2B onto the Llama-3 template dropped AlpacaEval 2.0 score from 41.2 to 28.8 (motivates why templates are stable/inferable)
Prompt-rewrite semantic fidelity: DIA rewrites keep mean cosine similarity 0.820 (AdvBench)/0.809 (HEx-PHI) vs ReNe's 0.458/0.506
Ablation (AdvBench, Guard-2): removing system prompt drops DIA-I on Llama-3-8B from 0.571 to 0.010; removing answer guidance drops DIA-II on Llama-2-7B from 0.467 to 0.113
baselines_compared: ReNe (prompt rewrite + scenario nesting; trades semantic integrity for ASR)
